body_html:
Page 1

BEARING 315
Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club
Jul/Aug 1985

Editor wanted

REWARDING WRITING EXPERIENCE AVAILABLE

"I enjoy reading newsletters of Orienteering events but sometimes they're boring or even confusing!" replied \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_. If your name fits in the blank, if you feel this way sometimes, then cheer up. You can do something to change things. Volunteer some time for writing the WIOL newsletter. It's easy. The newsletter for the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League provides info and feedback to the high school students who participate competitively among high schools. It provides an excellent introduction to orienteering for many teenagers of which the active ones continue their interest by joining "Cascade" after high school. The WIOL season runs from October to March. There will be approximately 6 newsletters for the season. Each newsletter would be the result of your creativity, writing, meet results collected at the meets or from Mike Schuh (who is tentatively the WIOL coordinator for '85-'86), your humor, paste-up, and trips to the copy mart. Don't worry if you've never done this before, you can get help from me, gladly. You say you want more info???!!!!!??!?! Call me,
Roger Sharp .....329-3675 or Mike Schuh .....783-3960.

I plan to volunteer my time to registration at the meets and course setting. Thanks for any time you can volunteer.

  • Roger Sharp

Pot-O'-lucks

The next Cascade O.C. potluck will be a combined Woodland Park relay "O" and pot-"O"-luck on 5 July 1985. We will start gathering around 5:00 PM at shelter #5 which is the 2nd shelter south of the lawn bowling area. Bring some "O" d'oeuvres to snack on since the mass start will not occur until after 6:00, promptly, I think! The course will most likely be a modified score course, but since it isn't designed yet, anything could happen. Teams will consist of 3 to 4 people assigned at the discretion of the Meet Director. As soon as the last person finishes we will orienteer from dish to dish at the potluck. So bring an "O" d'oeuvre, main dish, & some tall "O" tales for an evening of social orienteering.... Following that is the potluck you all have been waiting for on 16 August at 6:00 pm out at Knut Olson's Gold Creek Trout Farm in Woodinville. We all will have the opportunity to catch a trout or 2 and cook our catch over a barbecue. Bring the usual potluck dishes such as salads, bread, drink, etc. and those who don't want fish should be sure to bring a main course. The address is 15844-148th NE and the phone number is 483-1415. If you can get to St. Michelle Winery, you're almost there.

Page 2

Officer & Board Members

The officers and executive board members of the Cascade Orienteering Club are:

WIOL CoordinatorMike Schuh783-3960

EquipmentFrancis Smith634-1104
PublicityBo Powell232-5295

BEARING 315 contains results, a schedule, articles of interest contributed by members or selected from other sources and lots of material written by the editor, Harvey Friedman; letters to the editor are most welcome. Any correspondence to Cascade Orienteering Club or to this Newsletter should go to

P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, Wash. 98103

Other clubs wishing to receive BEARING 315 in exchange should send their newsletters directly to the above address.

Next Issue

All material to be published in the next issue must be received by 15 August the earlier, the better.

Club dues

Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving BEARING 315, the club newsletter, and seeing how your name might be misspelled in the results column. An application form is enclosed somewhere in this newsletter for your convenience. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Neil Cochran, the Everett family, the Fowler family, Peter Haynes, Kent Jameson, Erin Renshaw, the Reabold family, the Sprugel family, Tim Vargo.

Schedule assumptions

Not all meets listed in the schedule of events are hosted by Cascade Orienteering Club but I do not indicate the hosting clubs except on occasion. I do this to encourage all readers of this newsletter to consider attending any meet listed. Most are within a 10 hour drive of Seattle and the more people who go, the better the time for all. Part of the fun of orienteering is carpooling to a somewhat distance meet where we are the guests. Think about it.

Report on last club meeting
The last meeting of COC on Thursday, 16 May 1985 at the University Branch of the Seattle Public Library had a discussion of whether Cascade should host the 1986 United States Orienteering Federation convention and hold at least 1 day of "Class A" meet and preferably a day or 2 of something like canoe-O or night-O also. Those present voted against mainly because there were so few people present. Without 20 or more volunteers, the club leaders felt that it would be too much work for the club leaders. The program was an interesting discussion on course design led by Harvey Friedman. He prepared handouts to illustrate the general principles involved in putting together a good set of courses on our Pacific Northwest terrain, discussed how to actually plan and set the markers out, and how to determine good legs from bad. Other experienced course setters, particularly John Huth and Anne York, contributed much valuable criticism and comments for the bennefit of those who have never designed a course before. It was decided that this program succeeded so well that in the future we should have one writing correct control descriptions on the control description sheet both in English and in international symbols (that is those funny squiggles in those 8 columns on your 'clue' sheet). We who go to European meets appreciate the practise we get at loacal meets in using these international symbols.

Page 5

SCHEDULE OF EVENTS

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $2.50 for members and $3.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.

The San Jose Scene
85 people and sunshine showed up for the 1985 May "Class A" meet in San Jose, that was hosted by the Bay Area Orienteering Club. The course was the southern portion of Joseph Grant Park which is very hilly. Many times I was traversing steep hillsides and glad for the lightweight hiking shoes that come up over my ankles. The ground was extremely hard and there were dried ditches that were 3 feet wide by 4 feet deep suddenly appearing in front of me making it dangerously adventurous. Cold, sparkling "Calistoga" water greeted all finishers, as well as water available mid-course.

Bruce Wolfe was there to take 1st place in the M21-A class. From Tacoma, Gary McCausland received a ribbon for 2nd place in the M35-A class and Nathan Tharp took 1st place in M15-16-A class. I came in 4th place in the M-open class of which there were less than 12 entrants.

Gary invited everyone to a 1986 class A event being planned by the Nisqually Orienteers.

  • Roger Sharp

Potluck result

The Ooh-La-La Bruce McAlister "O" Potluck

It was a soon-to-be dark and stormy night. The sun was setting like a thin-skinned chocolate pudding.

Robert and Sandy Miller (bearing a three-gallon Eddie Bauer waterfowl painted tin of fresh, hot air popped without salt Orville Redenbacher corn), rang the bell at the close-to-St-Edward-park, surrounded-by-tulips house of Bruce McAlister. Inside the skittish Shetland Collie, Bruce's Bonnie, barked.

Anne York and Harvey Friedman (having born half a 2 kilo loaf of imported (from Canada) dark rye bread, a gallon jug of unpreserved cider and a 45-rpm-record-

Page 7

sized wheel of Camembert cheese) opened the door.

Our host, a book-plant-and-red-wine person, was putting a light dusting of mozzarella on a properly-made-with-ricotta-cheese lasagne into the oven.

Debbie Newell controlled our waiting-for-lasagna-palates with a bag of freshly purchased Grady's au gratin flavored, extra thick style chips. After a cameo appearance by Collin McAlister, our host's nephew, the enological drama began to uncork.

The first feature was an unpretentious Hungarian Rose, however, few having seen the dusty-bottle-filled wine rack were tempted. Punching in soon after were two Cabernet Sauvignons: a hearty yet unnature Nappa Valley label and a smoother but less robust oak flavored Quercas. Having determined that our palates were capable of discerning the subtler aromatics of a truly great vintage, our host uncorked a bottle of Chateau Mouton Rothschild circa 1967 (suggested retail value $20.00 per glass).

After the perfect pasta and vin extraordinaire, like the next control on a properly set white course - soon followed desert. It was a custard and cream, homemade, fresh strawberry gallon of gelato. As we five O-guests wrestled with the gelato scoop as if it were an entangled punch in a pit at a crowded common control, our host narrowly escaped injury as he rescued a paltry portion for himself.

As six became clearly all that one bottle of Rothschild, a pan of lasagne, and a gallon of gelato could support, comments about the skant COC member attendance took on a decidely now-we're-glad-more-didn't-show-up flavor, as when it's time to pick up controls on cold rainy morns.
-Robert-O

(This pretensious portrayal of potluck proceedings was contributed (as you might have guessed) by Robert Miller. Perhaps he will win the bad writing contest that inspired such an entertaining effort. -Ed.)

Cascade Chatter

Dave & Valerie Enger's wedding party was so much fun that at least 1 other Cascade member has decided to take the plunge (not really but it was such a good lead ). Long-time Cascade member Tony Paulson and Joan Benderson will soon to celebrate their wedding...Marina Tolmachova Waugh was awarded a National Endowment for the Humanities fellowship to study at UCLA inthe field of history this summer. We all will miss her at meets but hope she does well there and isn't too

Page 9

uncomfortable in the hot, dry climate....Harvey Friedman was at a 4 day computer conference in St. Louis where he presented a paper. Though busy, he did find time to visit with former Cascade O. C. members Bill & Judy Connett. (Why are the above 2 sentences in 3rd person? I, Harvey, am the one who writes the Cascade Chatter column.) Besides their little dog, Napper, they have another small addition to the family, a baby girl named Lillian who is just about a year old. Bill & Judy miss Seattle and will probably return for a visit this August sometime....Summertimes often brings other orienteering folks from outside our area, so anyone who might have room for someone for a night or 2, give a holler. Currently we expect a family of 5 from Norway to stop around 13 July on their way home from school in Irvine, California; a group of 4 Norwegians will be coming thru in either August or September; and according to his last letter, Ingemar will be here the 1st week in August....I almost forgot. Jan Vrana is back in Seattle again; he is currently staying with us at 6018 Sycamore NW, Seattle 98107 in case anyone wants to contact him. Because he works Saturdays, he doesn't get to as many meets as when he was just going to school last winter and spring....By the Ballard bike-O meet on 20 July, we should have available a new batch of Cascade Orienteering Club T-shirts for sale to members and others who want to buy them. The design is similiar to the previous on but with more letters (Seattle, Washington U.S.A.) and an actual outline of part of the Cascade mountain range now. Also the design will be much larger and centered instead of just on the left breast as before. The price might be $10 but I can't really remember whether the Board has decided yet or not, and anyway it will depend on the cost for the whole prodedure. We will have small, medium, large, and extra-large so we should be able to fit just about anyone in the club now....Robert and Sandy Miller could have worn the shirts if they had been available on their recent trip to the U.K. and Iceland. They managed to find an orienteering meet in England to participate in and had good times. Those curious enough can probably persuade them to show their maps or you can come to the 11 July club meeting where they will show slides and discuss the trip....Mike Schuh attended the USOF convention at Plymouth, N.H. as the Cascade representative. Any other member of Cascade who is also a USOF member and who intended to attend the 2-day Class A meet and the convention meeting could have voted some of Cascade's votes. If you want to go to the next one, let an officer ( preferably the president ) know....For those of you wanting a more formal instruction environment than we have a local meet, several courses on Orienteering will be available. Mike Schuh teaches thru the U. of W. Experimental College, Eileen Williams teaches in Lynnwood thru Lynnwood Parks & Recreation and there may be a course this autumn given somewhere in Seattle thru the Northwest YMCA. Anyone interested contact Mike or Eileen or send a letter to the newletter editor. Instructors for 1-day workshops and especially for enticing more high school coaches and students into WIOL next winter are urgently needed. There is too much work for too few volunteers....How can we attain greater attendance at club meetings? Do members prefer travelogue programs, orienteering programs such as Sture Carlsson's brilliant presentation of orienteering tips or Harvey's course design discussion session, or would you rather have no program at all and just a business meeting.

Page 10

What is the best day of the week for a meeting, should we have them immediately following a meet, or should we dispense with them entirely and just let the executive board dictate everything to the rest of the club members? What about pot-O-lucks? Since only 6 of us attended the last one should we no longer schedule any after those occur that are in this newsletter? Why have so many multi-year members not renewed their memberships? How can those of us who are active improve the club and especially get others to be active too? Here's a question for the WIOL participants. Should we have half the meets on Sundays so that those kids who work on Saturdays could have the opportunity to take part also.? Scribble out your ideas and give them to an officer the next time you go to a meet, meeting, or potluckand the executive board will try to make sense out of it all. Is this newsletter read by anyone or should we just send out a schedule and results?....If the trend shown by the following Auditors' Report continues, we soon will have no money left in the treasury and be unable to maintain our O events. Ideas on how to get more people to meets and on how to get more to join C.O.C. are most welcome.

What with the late start and early finish (including moving the 2nd master map for the Super Advanced Course) the day turned out rather well.

Report on last club meeting The last meeting of COC on Thursday, 16 May 1985 at the University Branch of the Seattle Public Library had a discussion of whether Cascade should host the 1986 United States Orienteering Federation convention and hold at least 1 day of "Class A" meet and preferably a day or 2 of something like canoe-O or night-O also. Those present voted against mainly because there were so few people present. Without 20 or more volunteers, the club leaders felt that it would be too much work for the club leaders. The program was an interesting discussion on course design led by Harvey Friedman. He prepared handouts to illustrate the general principles involved in putting together a good set of courses on our Pacific Northwest terrain, discussed how to actually plan and set the markers out, and how to determine good legs from bad. Other experienced course setters, particularly John Huth and Anne York, contributed much valuable criticism and comments for the bennefit of those who have never designed a course before. It was decided that this program succeeded so well that in the future we should have one writing correct control descriptions on the control description sheet both in English and in international symbols (that is those funny squiggles in those 8 columns on your 'clue' sheet). We who go to European meets appreciate the practise we get at loacal meets in using these international symbols.

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $2.50 for members and $3.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.

The San Jose Scene 85 people and sunshine showed up for the 1985 May "Class A" meet in San Jose, that was hosted by the Bay Area Orienteering Club. The course was the southern portion of Joseph Grant Park which is very hilly. Many times I was traversing steep hillsides and glad for the lightweight hiking shoes that come up over my ankles. The ground was extremely hard and there were dried ditches that were 3 feet wide by 4 feet deep suddenly appearing in front of me making it dangerously adventurous. Cold, sparkling "Calistoga" water greeted all finishers, as well as water available mid-course.

Bruce Wolfe was there to take 1st place in the M21-A class. From Tacoma, Gary McCausland received a ribbon for 2nd place in the M35-A class and Nathan Tharp took 1st place in M15-16-A class. I came in 4th place in the M-open class of which there were less than 12 entrants.
Gary invited everyone to a 1986 class A event being planned by the Nisqually Orienteers.
It was a soon-to-be dark and stormy night. The sun was setting like a thin-skinned chocolate pudding.

Robert and Sandy Miller (bearing a three-gallon Eddie Bauer waterfowl painted tin of fresh, hot air popped without salt Orville Redenbacher corn), rang the bell at the close-to-St-Edward-park, surrounded-by-tulips house of Bruce McAlister. Inside the skittish Shetland Collie, Bruce's Bonnie, barked.
Anne York and Harvey Friedman (having born half a 2 kilo loaf of imported (from Canada) dark rye bread, a gallon jug of unpreserved cider and a 45-rpm-record-
sized wheel of Camembert cheese) opened the door.
Our host, a book-plant-and-red-wine person, was putting a light dusting of mozzarella on a properly-made-with-ricotta-cheese lasagne into the oven.

Debbie Newell controlled our waiting-for-lasagna-palates with a bag of freshly purchased Grady's au gratin flavored, extra thick style chips. After a cameo appearance by Collin McAlister, our host's nephew, the enological drama began to uncork.

The first feature was an unpretentious Hungarian Rose, however, few having seen the dusty-bottle-filled wine rack were tempted. Punching in soon after were two Cabernet Sauvignons: a hearty yet unnature Nappa Valley label and a smoother but less robust oak flavored Quercas. Having determined that our palates were capable of discerning the subtler aromatics of a truly great vintage, our host uncorked a bottle of Chateau Mouton Rothschild circa 1967 (suggested retail value $20.00 per glass).

After the perfect pasta and vin extraordinaire, like the next control on a properly set white course - soon followed desert. It was a custard and cream, homemade, fresh strawberry gallon of gelato. As we five O-guests wrestled with the gelato scoop as if it were an entangled punch in a pit at a crowded common control, our host narrowly escaped injury as he rescued a paltry portion for himself.

As six became clearly all that one bottle of Rothschild, a pan of lasagne, and a gallon of gelato could support, comments about the skant COC member attendance took on a decidely now-we're-glad-more-didn't-show-up flavor, as when it's time to pick up controls on cold rainy morns. -Robert-O

(This pretensious portrayal of potluck proceedings was contributed (as you might have guessed) by Robert Miller. Perhaps he will win the bad writing contest that inspired such an entertaining effort. -Ed.)

Lincoln Park, 22 June 1985 Meet Director: Roger Sharp; Course design & set: Tim Vargo; Timing: Liz Anderson & Tim Vargo; Other help: Brad Payne & Francis Smith

Dave & Valerie Enger's wedding party was so much fun that at least 1 other Cascade member has decided to take the plunge (not really but it was such a good lead ). Long-time Cascade member Tony Paulson and Joan Benderson will soon to celebrate their wedding...Marina Tolmachova Waugh was awarded a National Endowment for the Humanities fellowship to study at UCLA inthe field of history this summer. We all will miss her at meets but hope she does well there and isn't too

uncomfortable in the hot, dry climate....Harvey Friedman was at a 4 day computer conference in St. Louis where he presented a paper. Though busy, he did find time to visit with former Cascade O. C. members Bill & Judy Connett. (Why are the above 2 sentences in 3rd person? I, Harvey, am the one who writes the Cascade Chatter column.) Besides their little dog, Napper, they have another small addition to the family, a baby girl named Lillian who is just about a year old. Bill & Judy miss Seattle and will probably return for a visit this August sometime....Summertimes often brings other orienteering folks from outside our area, so anyone who might have room for someone for a night or 2, give a holler. Currently we expect a family of 5 from Norway to stop around 13 July on their way home from school in Irvine, California; a group of 4 Norwegians will be coming thru in either August or September; and according to his last letter, Ingemar will be here the 1st week in August....I almost forgot. Jan Vrana is back in Seattle again; he is currently staying with us at 6018 Sycamore NW, Seattle 98107 in case anyone wants to contact him. Because he works Saturdays, he doesn't get to as many meets as when he was just going to school last winter and spring....By the Ballard bike-O meet on 20 July, we should have available a new batch of Cascade Orienteering Club T-shirts for sale to members and others who want to buy them. The design is similiar to the previous on but with more letters (Seattle, Washington U.S.A.) and an actual outline of part of the Cascade mountain range now. Also the design will be much larger and centered instead of just on the left breast as before. The price might be $10 but I can't really remember whether the Board has decided yet or not, and anyway it will depend on the cost for the whole prodedure. We will have small, medium, large, and extra-large so we should be able to fit just about anyone in the club now....Robert and Sandy Miller could have worn the shirts if they had been available on their recent trip to the U.K. and Iceland. They managed to find an orienteering meet in England to participate in and had good times. Those curious enough can probably persuade them to show their maps or you can come to the 11 July club meeting where they will show slides and discuss the trip....Mike Schuh attended the USOF convention at Plymouth, N.H. as the Cascade representative. Any other member of Cascade who is also a USOF member and who intended to attend the 2-day Class A meet and the convention meeting could have voted some of Cascade's votes. If you want to go to the next one, let an officer ( preferably the president ) know....For those of you wanting a more formal instruction environment than we have a local meet, several courses on Orienteering will be available. Mike Schuh teaches thru the U. of W. Experimental College, Eileen Williams teaches in Lynnwood thru Lynnwood Parks & Recreation and there may be a course this autumn given somewhere in Seattle thru the Northwest YMCA. Anyone interested contact Mike or Eileen or send a letter to the newletter editor. Instructors for 1-day workshops and especially for enticing more high school coaches and students into WIOL next winter are urgently needed. There is too much work for too few volunteers....How can we attain greater attendance at club meetings? Do members prefer travelogue programs, orienteering programs such as Sture Carlsson's brilliant presentation of orienteering tips or Harvey's course design discussion session, or would you rather have no program at all and just a business meeting.

What is the best day of the week for a meeting, should we have them immediately following a meet, or should we dispense with them entirely and just let the executive board dictate everything to the rest of the club members? What about pot-O-lucks? Since only 6 of us attended the last one should we no longer schedule any after those occur that are in this newsletter? Why have so many multi-year members not renewed their memberships? How can those of us who are active improve the club and especially get others to be active too? Here's a question for the WIOL participants. Should we have half the meets on Sundays so that those kids who work on Saturdays could have the opportunity to take part also.? Scribble out your ideas and give them to an officer the next time you go to a meet, meeting, or potluckand the executive board will try to make sense out of it all. Is this newsletter read by anyone or should we just send out a schedule and results?....If the trend shown by the following Auditors' Report continues, we soon will have no money left in the treasury and be unable to maintain our O events. Ideas on how to get more people to meets and on how to get more to join C.O.C. are most welcome.

Auditors' report and Income statement Anne York and Sandy Kish Miller have examined the books of the Cascade Orienteering Club and have verified existing balances. They commend 1984 treasurer Chris Hawkins on a fine job.
Ending Balance: 546.49 Total Cash Decrease of: 752.76
